subject: UX & Research
title: Wikipedia's Category System
description: A 60-second lesson on Wikipedia's Category System.

hook.text: Wikipedia has over 2 million categories. Articles belong to multiple ones. It's the world's largest folksonomy.

buildup.text: Editors create and assign categories freely. The system is decentralized, organic, and sometimes contradictory.

discovery.text: Navigating Wikipedia by categories is nearly impossible. Users rely on search and internal links instead.

twist.text: Despite messy categories, Wikipedia works because internal links create a dense web. Articles connect to dozens more.

climax.text: Wikipedia proves perfect taxonomy isn't needed if links are strong enough. Connections beat categories.

punchline.text: Links beat categories. Dense connections make perfect taxonomy unnecessary.

quiz.question: Why does Wikipedia work despite having messy, inconsistent categories?
options: Dense internal linking between articles creates strong connections that compensate for weak taxonomy
Users don't actually read Wikipedia articles
